title: MATH 244: Intermediate Data Science Project
text:
Welcome to Anders Hansen and Miles Sondergaard Jensen’s report for the MATH 244: Intermediate Data Science final project associated with Vassar College.





Image from rawpixel

title: Introduction
text:
[cite_start]In the music industry, there is a long-standing debate: is a song’s success driven by its tangible musical characteristics, or is it a product of “unmeasurables” like aggressive marketing, pure luck, or timing? [cite: 67]
[cite_start]Our project, “The Danish Club,” seeks to answer this by analyzing a comprehensive sample of hit and non-hit songs released between 2010 and 2019. [cite: 67] [cite_start]We define a “hit” specifically as any song that made an appearance on the Billboard Hot 100. [cite: 67]


We chose to focus on the 2010s for several reasons: * [cite_start]Formative Years: This decade represents the childhood and teenage years for many in our generation, making the music personally resonant. [cite: 67] * [cite_start]Lasting Impact: The music from this era was exceptionally high-quality, with many tracks still frequently charting or receiving heavy rotation today. [cite: 67] * [cite_start]Shifting Trends: The criteria for what makes a song “successful” appeared to undergo significant shifts during this ten-year window. [cite: 67]



[cite_start]The primary objective of this study is to predict the success of 2010s songs based on their specific audio characteristics. [cite: 67] [cite_start]We aimed to build a classification model capable of differentiating hit songs from non-hit songs with at least 50% accuracy. [cite: 67]



Going into our modeling phase, we developed two sets of expectations regarding Spotify’s audio features:

[cite_start]Predictors of Success: We hypothesized that higher danceability, valence (musical positiveness), and loudness would significantly increase a song’s likelihood of becoming a hit. [cite: 105] [cite_start]Conversely, we expected lower duration, acousticness, speechiness, and instrumentalness to be associated with chart success. [cite: 105]
[cite_start]Neutral Elements: We predicted that features like key, tempo, mode, and whether a song is explicit would have little to no measurable effect on a song’s probability of charting. [cite: 105]


Next, we will dive into the Data and Cleaning process to show how we narrowed down nearly a million observations into a balanced dataset for our analysis.
